messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Nonlinear Structural Equation Modeling in R
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Nonlinear Structural Equation Modeling in R
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Enhance your statistical toolkit with advanced modeling techniques for complex observational data. Nonlinear Structural Equation Modeling is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 11 with Holmes Finch (Department of Educational Psychology - Ball State University). Epidemiological researchers can apply these advanced methods to model complex, non-additive exposure-outcome pathways and latent health states more accurately. By integrating nonlinear structural equation modeling into your R workflow, you can better estimate threshold effects, latent interactions, and curvilinear growth trajectories in longitudinal cohort data. This allows for more robust causal mediation analyses and realistic measurement models when analyzing observational health datasets.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/nonlinear-structural-equation-modeling) to reserve your place before it starts, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Overview of the GDIS Hub
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Enhance your epidemiological research by integrating robust, disaggregated statistical data into your analytical workflows. Overview of the GDIS Hub (Free Seminar) is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 23, led by Fran?ois Marshall (Statistics Canada). This upcoming seminar introduces the Gender, Diversity and Inclusion Statistics (GDIS) Hub as a major entry point for disaggregated and intersectional statistical evidence. Epidemiologists can leverage these robust, high-quality public data resources to move beyond aggregate summaries and illuminate health heterogeneity across intersecting social categories. By mastering the Hub's structure and data holdings, you can integrate official statistics directly into your R-based epidemiological models to improve the precision of your empirical claims and strengthen peer-reviewed publications.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/overview-of-the-gdis-hub-free-seminar) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Synthesizing vast bodies of evidence efficiently is becoming increasingly critical for modern scientific research. AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 10 and 12 with Moses Boudourides (Data Science Graduate Program, School of Professional Studies, Northwestern University). Epidemiologists can leverage these AI-assisted workflows to rapidly screen and synthesize massive volumes of clinical and observational study literature. By integrating open bibliographic APIs and machine learning models, you can streamline the screening and data extraction phases of your meta-analyses while maintaining the strict transparency required for public health publications. This allows your research team to focus on interpreting epidemiological patterns and running robust statistical analyses in R rather than getting bogged down in manual database searches.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/ai-assisted-systematic-reviews-and-meta) to join before enrollment closes, and please pass this to colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] BART models for species distribution modelling (SDMs)
messages.subject_clean: BART models for species distribution modelling (SDMs)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
we are pleased to announce our online course: Species Distribution Modelling with Bayesian Additive Regression Tree (BART) methods
 

Dates: 1?3 July - Daily schedule: 15:00?18:00 (Berlin time)


 
 
 
Course website: https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/barts/
 
 
 
 
 
This course introduces BART models for species distribution modelling (SDMs) and shows how they can improve prediction and interpretation compared to traditional approaches.
 
Using R and the embarcadero and dbarts packages, participants will learn the full SDM workflow: from predictor selection and model training to evaluation, interpretation, and prediction in new environments: 
 
Introduction to SDMs and BART theory
Hands-on modelling in R (embarcadero, dbarts)
Predictor selection, model fitting, and evaluation
Interpretation of effects and spatial predictions
Advanced topics including random-intercept BARTs
 
 
 
We look forward to welcoming you!
 
Best regards,
 
 
 
Carlo
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Handling Non-Normal Traits in IRT Models
messages.subject_clean: Handling Non-Normal Traits in IRT Models
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Enhance your statistical toolkit with advanced measurement techniques for non-standard data distributions. Handling Non-Normal Traits in IRT Models (Free Seminar) is a seminar livestreaming July 2, led by Holmes Finch (Department of Educational Psychology - Ball State University). This free seminar introduces robust estimation techniques for item response theory models when latent traits depart from standard normality assumptions. Epidemiologists can apply these methods in R to more accurately analyze skewed health surveys, patient-reported outcome measures, and complex diagnostic data. By implementing these reproducible workflows, you can prevent parameter distortion and ensure more reliable statistical inferences in your public health research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/handling-non-normal-traits-in-irt-models) to join the livestream, and please pass this along to colleagues and students who may find it useful!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Spatial Data and R as a GIS
messages.subject_clean: Spatial Data and R as a GIS
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Enhance your research capabilities by integrating advanced spatial analysis into your current data workflows. Introduction to Spatial Data and R as a GIS led by Nick Bearman (University College London and Geospatial Training Solutions) is now available as an on-demand streaming seminar. Epidemiologists can leverage spatial data and R to map disease transmission patterns and identify localized health disparities in their communities. By mastering R as a GIS, you will be able to automate the generation of choropleth maps and integrate spatial covariates directly into your epidemiological models. This on-demand seminar provides the practical skills needed to transition from basic statistical analysis to sophisticated spatial visualizations that advance public health research.

Access this seminar (https://instats.org/seminar/introduction-to-spatial-data-and-r-as-a-3) whenever convenient and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Workshop: Markov and Hidden Markov Models with Applications in R (1–4 September, online)
messages.subject_clean: Workshop: Markov and Hidden Markov Models with Applications in R (1–4 September, online)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are pleased to announce our upcoming online course "Markov and Hidden Markov Models with Applications in R", taking place 1?4 September.
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/markov-and-hidden-markov-models-with-applications-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/markov-and-hidden-markov-models-with-applications-in-r/ ) 
 
 
This course provides a practical introduction to Markov and Hidden Markov Models for the analysis of longitudinal data. Participants will learn the theoretical foundations of these models and gain hands-on experience implementing them in R using the LMest package.
 
 
Topics covered include:
 
Discrete latent variable models.
Markov and Hidden Markov Models.
Longitudinal data analysis.
Model estimation and selection procedures.
Handling continuous, categorical, and incomplete longitudinal data.
Bootstrap methods for uncertainty assessment.
Applications using the R package LMest.
 
 
Best regards,
Carlo

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Exploratory Analysis of Complex Datasets
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Exploratory Analysis of Complex Datasets
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Enhance your statistical workflows with advanced data analysis techniques starting next week. Exploratory Analysis of Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 is a seminar livestreaming June 17 with Nikolay Oskolkov (Lund University). Epidemiologists can apply these advanced dimensionality reduction and clustering techniques to uncover hidden patterns in highly complex, multi-variable health datasets. By integrating R and Python workflows with modern AI tools, researchers can streamline the processing of large-scale observational and time-series epidemiological data. This allows for more robust anomaly detection and precise subgroup identification to directly advance public health insights.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/exploratory-analysis-of-complex-datasets-3) to reserve your place before it starts, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Practical Meta-Analysis for Beginners
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Practical Meta-Analysis for Beginners
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Registration is closing soon for our upcoming interactive research methods workshop. Practical Meta-Analysis for Beginners is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 22 and 24 with Centre for Applied Statistics Courses (CASC) (University College London). This upcoming livestream seminar offers a comprehensive introduction to synthesizing evidence across multiple studies, a critical skill for integrating diverse epidemiological findings. By mastering fixed and random-effects models, you can systematically aggregate effect estimates to resolve contradictions in existing literature. The optional practical sessions in R will enable you to directly apply these meta-analytic methods to your own datasets, enhancing the rigor of your epidemiological research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/practical-meta-analysis-for-beginners-1) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Overview of the GDIS Hub
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Overview of the GDIS Hub
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - An upcoming live seminar offers valuable insights into a major public data resource for empirical researchers. Overview of the GDIS Hub (Free Seminar) is a seminar livestreaming June 23 with Fran?ois Marshall (Statistics Canada). This upcoming seminar provides an expert-guided introduction to the Gender, Diversity and Inclusion Statistics (GDIS) Hub and its disaggregated data holdings. Epidemiologists can leverage these structured public data resources to incorporate robust intersectional variables directly into their R-based statistical models and survival analyses. By mastering the Hub's organization, researchers will be able to better analyze health disparities and strengthen the empirical foundation of their peer-reviewed publications.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/overview-of-the-gdis-hub-free-seminar) to join before enrollment closes, and please pass this to colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Species Distribution Models with BART
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
There is still time to join our upcoming online course and take your species distribution modelling (SDM) skills to the next level with us: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/barts/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/barts/ ) 
 
Dates: 1-3 July
 
In this hands-on course, participants will learn how to apply Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART) to species distribution modelling and explore powerful R workflows using the embarcadero and dbarts packages.
 
The course will cover practical approaches for building, interpreting, and applying BART-based SDMs, providing participants with new tools for ecological modelling and prediction.
 
Join us online and discover how Bayesian machine learning can enhance your SDM workflows!
 
Best regards,
Carlo

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Building Autonomous AI Agents for Research
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Building Autonomous AI Agents for Research
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - As computational demands grow, researchers need more efficient ways to manage complex data workflows. Building Autonomous AI Agents for Scientific Research is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 26?27 with Renan Maestri (Department of Ecology, Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Sul). This upcoming livestream seminar demonstrates how to build and deploy autonomous AI agents directly within R-based environments. Epidemiologists can apply these agentic workflows to automate complex data-cleaning pipelines, orchestrate statistical modeling tasks, and systematically retrieve public health literature. By shifting from simple chat prompts to self-planning agents, you can significantly scale your research productivity while maintaining the strict reproducibility required for epidemiological studies.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/building-autonomous-ai-agents-for-scient) to register now, and we'd appreciate you sharing this with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
